Numerical Linear Algebra

The Singular Value Decomposition

The Singular Value Decomposition

The previous chapter left off with a cliffhanger: eigenvalue decomposition is a beautiful way to understand how a matrix acts, but it only works for square matrices, and even then only for the well-behaved ones. This chapter resolves that limitation completely. The Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is the factorization that works for every matrix — square or rectangular, symmetric or not, diagonalizable or not — and in doing so it becomes the single most useful decomposition in this course, tying together everything from projectors and QR factorization to condition numbers and least squares.

The Limitations of the Eigenvalue Decomposition

Recall the eigenvalue decomposition (EVD) from the previous chapter: if A∈Rn×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}A∈Rn×n is diagonalizable, then A=XΛX−1.A = X\Lambda X^{-1}.A=XΛX−1.

A particularly nice case arose when AAA was real and symmetric — there, the eigenvectors could always be chosen orthonormal, giving A=QΛQTA = Q\Lambda Q^TA=QΛQT with QQQ orthogonal and Λ\LambdaΛ diagonal.

Important refinement

The factorization A=QΛQTA = Q\Lambda Q^TA=QΛQT holds for real symmetric matrices. If AAA is additionally symmetric positive definite (SPD), all of its eigenvalues are strictly positive: λi>0\lambda_i > 0λi​>0 for every iii.

As clean as this is, it rests on three restrictive assumptions, and each one rules out matrices we care about:

  • AAA must be square. Least-squares problems, by their very nature, involve tall rectangular matrices — EVD has nothing to say about them directly.
  • Orthogonal diagonalization needs symmetry. Most matrices in practice are not symmetric.
  • Even square matrices can fail to diagonalize. So-called defective matrices simply don’t have enough independent eigenvectors to build XXX at all.

The Necessity of SVD

What do we do when AAA is rectangular, non-symmetric, or defective? This is exactly the gap the SVD fills — a factorization defined for every matrix A∈Rn×mA \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times m}A∈Rn×m, with no restrictions at all.

Two ways to motivate SVD
  1. Algebraic: Every matrix, no matter how badly behaved, is orthogonally equivalent to a simple diagonal-like matrix.
  2. Geometric: Any linear map sends the unit sphere to a hyperellipse; the SVD identifies that hyperellipse’s principal axes and their lengths.
Orthogonal equivalence

Two matrices A,B∈Rn×mA, B \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times m}A,B∈Rn×m are equivalent if there exist invertible matrices X∈Rn×nX\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}X∈Rn×n and Y∈Rm×mY\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}Y∈Rm×m with A=XBYA = XBYA=XBY. If XXX and YYY are both orthogonal, AAA and BBB are orthogonally equivalent.

The SVD is precisely the statement that every matrix is orthogonally equivalent to a rectangular diagonal matrix — no symmetry, no square shape, and no diagonalizability required.

The Singular Value Decomposition: Formal Statement

Singular Value Decomposition

Let A∈Rn×mA\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}A∈Rn×m have rank rrr. There exist orthogonal matrices U∈Rn×nU\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}U∈Rn×n and V∈Rm×mV\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}V∈Rm×m, and a rectangular diagonal matrix Σ∈Rn×m\Sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}Σ∈Rn×m, such that A=UΣVT.A = U\Sigma V^T.A=UΣVT. The diagonal entries of Σ\SigmaΣ are the singular values σ1≥σ2≥⋯≥σr>0\sigma_1\ge\sigma_2\ge\cdots\ge\sigma_r > 0σ1​≥σ2​≥⋯≥σr​>0, with all remaining diagonal entries equal to zero.

The pieces of the decomposition. UUU’s columns u1,…,unu_1,\dots,u_nu1​,…,un​ are the left singular vectors; VVV‘s columns v1,…,vmv_1,\dots,v_mv1​,…,vm​ are the right singular vectors; Σ\SigmaΣ holds the singular values in descending order along its diagonal. Transposing gives the equivalent form AT=VΣTUTA^T = V\Sigma^TU^TAT=VΣTUT — the SVD of ATA^TAT is just the SVD of AAA with the roles of UUU and VVV swapped.

Where the singular values come from

The SVD isn’t a brand-new idea invented from scratch — it’s built directly on top of the eigen-decomposition machinery from the previous chapter, applied to a pair of matrices constructed from AAA that are always symmetric, no matter what AAA looks like.

Singular values and eigenvalues

For any A∈Rn×mA\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}A∈Rn×m:

  • ATAA^TAATA is symmetric positive semidefinite in Rm×m\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}Rm×m.
  • AATAA^TAAT is symmetric positive semidefinite in Rn×n\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}Rn×n.
  • ATAA^TAATA and AATAA^TAAT share the same nonzero eigenvalues.
  • Those shared nonzero eigenvalues are exactly the squared singular values σ12,…,σr2\sigma_1^2,\dots,\sigma_r^2σ12​,…,σr2​.

This is the resolution of last chapter’s restriction: AAA itself might not be symmetric or even square, but ATAA^TAATA and AATAA^TAAT always are — so the well-behaved spectral decomposition QΛQTQ\Lambda Q^TQΛQT from the SPD case always applies to them, even when it can’t be applied to AAA directly.

Concretely: if ATAvi=σi2viA^TAv_i = \sigma_i^2v_iATAvi​=σi2​vi​, then viv_ivi​ is a right singular vector, and for σi>0\sigma_i>0σi​>0 the matching left singular vector is defined by ui=Avi/σiu_i = Av_i/\sigma_iui​=Avi​/σi​. This produces the pair of relations that govern everything else in this chapter: Avi=σiui,ATui=σivi.Av_i = \sigma_iu_i, \qquad A^Tu_i = \sigma_iv_i.Avi​=σi​ui​,ATui​=σi​vi​.

Geometric Interpretation of SVD

A linear map in three steps

The factorization A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT isn’t just algebra — it’s a recipe for what AAA does to a vector, broken into three simple stages:

  1. VTV^TVT — an orthogonal rotation or reflection in the input space Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm.
  2. Σ\SigmaΣ — a pure stretch or compression along the coordinate axes (no rotation at all).
  3. UUU — an orthogonal rotation or reflection into the output space Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.

Every linear map, however complicated it looks in its original coordinates, is secretly just “rotate, stretch along axes, rotate again” once you view it through the right pair of orthonormal bases. This is a direct generalization of the reflectors and rotators from earlier in the course — there, orthogonal matrices were built by hand to introduce zeros; here, UUU and VVV arise naturally as the coordinate systems in which AAA‘s action becomes as simple as possible.

The unit sphere becomes a hyperellipse

Let SSS be the unit sphere in the domain Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm. Applying AAA to SSS produces a hyperellipse in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn. The right singular vectors viv_ivi​ mark the principal axes in the input space; the singular values σi\sigma_iσi​ set the lengths of the resulting semiaxes; and the left singular vectors uiu_iui​ set their directions in the output space. Since Avi=σiuiAv_i = \sigma_iu_iAvi​=σi​ui​, each unit vector viv_ivi​ is mapped to a vector of length exactly σi\sigma_iσi​, pointing along uiu_iui​ — the SVD literally reads off the shape of A(S)A(S)A(S) axis by axis.

SVD and the Four Fundamental Subspaces

One of the SVD’s most powerful features is that it hands you explicit orthonormal bases for all four fundamental subspaces of AAA at once — no extra work required beyond the factorization itself. The action on the singular vectors splits cleanly at index rrr (the rank of AAA):

Avi={σiui,i=1,…,r0,i=r+1,…,mATui={σivi,i=1,…,r0,i=r+1,…,nAv_i = \begin{cases}\sigma_iu_i, & i=1,\dots,r\\ 0, & i=r+1,\dots,m\end{cases} \qquad A^Tu_i = \begin{cases}\sigma_iv_i, & i=1,\dots,r\\ 0, & i=r+1,\dots,n\end{cases}Avi​={σi​ui​,0,​i=1,…,ri=r+1,…,m​ATui​={σi​vi​,0,​i=1,…,ri=r+1,…,n​

This immediately yields:

  • Column space / range of AAA: Range⁡(A)=span⁡{u1,…,ur}\operatorname{Range}(A) = \operatorname{span}\{u_1,\dots,u_r\}Range(A)=span{u1​,…,ur​}
  • Null space of AAA: Null⁡(A)=span⁡{vr+1,…,vm}\operatorname{Null}(A) = \operatorname{span}\{v_{r+1},\dots,v_m\}Null(A)=span{vr+1​,…,vm​}
  • Row space / range of ATA^TAT: Range⁡(AT)=span⁡{v1,…,vr}\operatorname{Range}(A^T) = \operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots,v_r\}Range(AT)=span{v1​,…,vr​}
  • Left null space: Null⁡(AT)=span⁡{ur+1,…,un}\operatorname{Null}(A^T) = \operatorname{span}\{u_{r+1},\dots,u_n\}Null(AT)=span{ur+1​,…,un​}

This is exactly the same subspace structure that the full QR factorization revealed a chapter ago — there, Q^\hat QQ^​‘s columns spanned Range⁡(A)\operatorname{Range}(A)Range(A) and the leftover columns of QQQ spanned Null⁡(AT)\operatorname{Null}(A^T)Null(AT). SVD recovers that same picture, but now for both the row and column sides simultaneously, and with the added bonus of the singular values telling you exactly how much AAA stretches each direction.

Fundamental subspace relations

Range⁡(A)=Null⁡(AT)⊥,Range⁡(AT)=Null⁡(A)⊥\operatorname{Range}(A) = \operatorname{Null}(A^T)^\perp, \qquad \operatorname{Range}(A^T) = \operatorname{Null}(A)^\perpRange(A)=Null(AT)⊥,Range(AT)=Null(A)⊥

Full, Reduced, and Truncated SVD

Not every application needs the complete matrices UUU and VVV. The SVD comes in three practical sizes, each discarding a bit more of the “wasted” structure.

Full SVD. The complete decomposition A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT, with U∈Rn×nU\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}U∈Rn×n, Σ∈Rn×m\Sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}Σ∈Rn×m, V∈Rm×mV\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}V∈Rm×m — analogous to full QR, where QQQ was padded out to be square even though only part of it was strictly needed.

Reduced (economy) SVD. If rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A) = rrank(A)=r, the zero rows/columns of Σ\SigmaΣ contribute nothing to the matrix product, so we can drop them along with the corresponding singular vectors: A=UrΣrVrT,Ur∈Rn×r, Σr∈Rr×r, Vr∈Rm×r.A = U_r\Sigma_rV_r^T, \qquad U_r\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times r},\ \Sigma_r\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times r},\ V_r\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times r}.A=Ur​Σr​VrT​,Ur​∈Rn×r, Σr​∈Rr×r, Vr​∈Rm×r. This is the direct analogue of the thin QR factorization from two chapters ago — the same idea of keeping only the columns that actually participate in reconstructing AAA.

Truncated rank-kkk approximation. Keeping only the k≤rk \le rk≤r largest singular values (and discarding the rest, not just the zero ones) gives a lower-rank matrix that approximates AAA: A≈UkΣkVkT.A \approx U_k\Sigma_kV_k^T.A≈Uk​Σk​VkT​.

Why truncation matters

Truncated SVD is the foundation of PCA, data compression, low-rank approximation, and denoising. It gives the best possible rank-kkk approximation of AAA, in both the spectral and Frobenius norms — no other rank-kkk matrix can do better by either measure.

Matrix Properties and Applications

Matrix norms, revisited

Because orthogonal matrices preserve Euclidean length (the property used throughout every chapter since projectors were first introduced), the overall “size” of AAA as a transformation is completely determined by its singular values.

Spectral norm and the largest singular value

∥A∥2=σ1\|A\|_2 = \sigma_1∥A∥2​=σ1​

The Frobenius norm — introduced back when we first discussed matrix norms — also has a clean expression in terms of singular values, using the cyclic invariance of the trace together with the fact that orthogonal matrices leave the trace unchanged: ∥A∥F=(trace⁡(ATA))1/2=σ12+σ22+⋯+σr2.\|A\|_F = \big(\operatorname{trace}(A^TA)\big)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\cdots+\sigma_r^2}.∥A∥F​=(trace(ATA))1/2=σ12​+σ22​+⋯+σr2​​.

Matrix condition number, revisited

Recall the condition number κ2(A)=∥A∥2∥A−1∥2\kappa_2(A) = \|A\|_2\|A^{-1}\|_2κ2​(A)=∥A∥2​∥A−1∥2​ from the numerical-stability chapter. The SVD makes this quantity completely explicit. If A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT, then A−1=VΣ−1UTA^{-1} = V\Sigma^{-1}U^TA−1=VΣ−1UT, so the singular values of A−1A^{-1}A−1 are simply the reciprocals 1/σ1,…,1/σn1/\sigma_1,\dots,1/\sigma_n1/σ1​,…,1/σn​ of those of AAA. This gives ∥A−1∥2=1/σn\|A^{-1}\|_2 = 1/\sigma_n∥A−1∥2​=1/σn​, and therefore:

Condition number in terms of singular values

κ2(A)=σ1σn\kappa_2(A) = \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_n}κ2​(A)=σn​σ1​​

Interpretation

A large condition number means a large gap between the biggest and smallest singular values — geometrically, the hyperellipse A(S)A(S)A(S) is extremely elongated along one axis and nearly flat along another. This near-degeneracy is exactly what makes the system numerically sensitive: small perturbations in the data can produce large changes in the solution.

Least squares: why SVD and QR dominate the normal equations

This closes the loop all the way back to the least-squares chapters. Recall the normal equations ATAx=ATbA^TAx = A^TbATAx=ATb, and the recurring warning that forming ATAA^TAATA explicitly is numerically dangerous. The SVD makes precisely why fully transparent: since the singular values of ATAA^TAATA are the squares of the singular values of AAA, κ2(ATA)=σ12σr2=κ2(A)2.\kappa_2(A^TA) = \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_r^2} = \kappa_2(A)^2.κ2​(ATA)=σr2​σ12​​=κ2​(A)2.

Numerical consequence

Forming ATAA^TAATA squares the condition number, amplifying floating-point error exactly as the numerical-stability chapter warned. This is the precise, singular-value-level explanation for why QR and SVD are preferred over the normal equations in practice.

Summary of Core Identities

ConceptEquation
FactorizationA=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT
Vector mappingAvi=σiuiandATui=σiviAv_i = \sigma_iu_i \quad\text{and}\quad A^Tu_i = \sigma_iv_iAvi​=σi​ui​andATui​=σi​vi​
Eigenvalue linkATAvi=σi2viandAATui=σi2uiA^TAv_i = \sigma_i^2v_i \quad\text{and}\quad AA^Tu_i = \sigma_i^2u_iATAvi​=σi2​vi​andAATui​=σi2​ui​
Spectral norm∥A∥2=σ1\|A\|_2 = \sigma_1∥A∥2​=σ1​
Conditioningκ2(A)=σmax⁡(A)σmin⁡(A)\kappa_2(A) = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}(A)}{\sigma_{\min}(A)}κ2​(A)=σmin​(A)σmax​(A)​
Main idea

The SVD is the most general and most useful matrix decomposition in this course:

  • It works for every matrix — no square, symmetric, or diagonalizable requirement.
  • It reveals the geometry of a linear map as rotate–stretch–rotate.
  • It gives orthonormal bases for all four fundamental subspaces at once.
  • It explains matrix norms, conditioning, PCA, compression, and — closing the loop with the very first least-squares chapter — exactly why QR-based methods are the numerically safe choice.
